check_2023-03-10_2023-03-17.narrative:
---

**TL;DR:** Check TPV spiked +56.3% (+$496K/day) from Mar 10–17 with the increase distributed broadly across every segment roughly in proportion to baseline share, pointing to a systemic, calendar/cycle-driven surge rather than a targeted event.

**Ranked Hypotheses:**

1. **Systemic mid-month billing/AP cycle surge (e.g., quarter-end pull-forward or mid-March AP run)** — Confidence: High
   - Evidence: Every dimension's deltas track baseline share almost perfectly: merchant_industry deltas range 9.6%–36.2% (matching baseline mix), payer_industry 9.7%–30.4%, payer_size enterprise 49.9% / mid_market 35.2% / smb 15.0% (baseline shares ~50/35/15). No single segment exceeds 50% of delta. Top interaction (enterprise × enterprise) is only 24.8%, again proportional to baseline.
   - Mechanism: A platform-wide timing event — mid-March AP cycles, pre-quarter-end vendor payments, or a batch of large invoices being paid by check — lifts all customer cohorts uniformly. Check is the legacy rail for large B2B AP, so a cycle effect lands disproportionately on enterprise payers/merchants simply because they dominate the baseline.

2. **Tax-season / 1099 & contractor settlement bulge** — Confidence: Medium
   - Evidence: Spike is concentrated in mid-March (pre-April-15 cycle), and the largest absolute contributors are professional_services merchants (+$180K/day, 36.2%) and healthcare merchants (+$151K/day, 30.4%) — both heavy users of check for contractor, partner, and provider payouts. Manufacturing payers also surged +$126K/day (25.4%), consistent with vendor true-ups.
   - Mechanism: Annual tax/reporting deadlines drive a temporary lift in check-based B2B disbursements (bonuses, partner distributions, provider settlements) that fades after mid-April. Effect is broad because it touches many industries simultaneously.

3. **Large enterprise customer concentration (a few big payers running larger-than-usual check runs)** — Confidence: Low–Medium
   - Evidence: Enterprise merchants drove 49.2% and enterprise payers 49.9% of the delta, with enterprise×enterprise alone at 24.8% of total. Loyal (28.2%) + established (28.8%) tenure buckets dominate, while new_0_3mo is just 4.4%.
   - Mechanism: A handful of long-tenured enterprise relationships running unusually large check batches could mimic a systemic pattern. However, the proportional distribution across industries argues against a single-customer driver — more likely many enterprise customers moving together.

4. **Reporting/backfill artifact (delayed check postings cleared in this window)** — Confidence: Low
   - Evidence: Check has a 14-day settlement window; a batch of previously pending checks marking `is_complete = TRUE` in this window could inflate observed TPV uniformly across segments.
   - Mechanism: Settlement/ingestion lag clearing en masse would look systemic. Worth ruling out before treating the spike as real demand.

**Most Diagnostic Dimension:** `payer_tenure_bucket`
The near-perfect proportionality across tenure buckets (every cohort contributing roughly its baseline share, with no new-account spike and no loyal-customer drop) is the strongest signal that this is an environmental/calendar event affecting the existing book uniformly, not an onboarding wave or churn-driven shift.

**Key Segment Findings:**
- `merchant_industry` — **professional_services**: 36.2% of total delta (+$179.7K/day) — largest absolute contributor, consistent with partner/contractor disbursement cycles
- `merchant_industry` — **healthcare**: 30.4% of total delta (+$150.9K/day) — provider/claims settlement check volume
- `payer_size` — **enterprise**: 49.9% of total delta (+$247.7K/day) — but matches baseline enterprise share (~50%), so proportional not concentrated
- `payer_tenure_bucket` — **new_0_3mo**: only 4.4% (+$21.8K/day) — rules out onboarding-driven or new-account fraud
- Top interaction: `merchant_size × payer_size` — **enterprise × enterprise**: 24.8% of delta (+$123K/day) — large but proportional to its baseline weight; no true intersection concentration

**Recommended Next Steps:**
- [ ] Pull daily check TPV for Mar 10–17 by issuing date vs. settlement date to rule out a settlement/backfill artifact (settlement window = 14 days)
- [ ] Run a top-20 payer/merchant concentration query for the anomaly window — confirm the enterprise contribution is many customers, not 2–3 outliers
- [ ] Compare to March 2022 check TPV to test the seasonal/tax-season hypothesis (mid-March AP + 1099 cycle)
- [ ] Check whether `regular_ach` or `two_day_ach` show any offsetting dip in the same window — if not, this is incremental volume, not rail substitution
- [ ] Notify Head of Payments and Treasury: confirm float/clearing capacity for the next 1–2 weeks given the +56% check volume, and flag to Finance if the lift looks sustained past Apr 15

---

regular_ach_2023-09-15_2023-09-19.narrative:
---

**TL;DR:** Regular ACH TPV dropped -8.1% (-$211.5K/day) from Sept 15–19 in a near-perfectly uniform pattern across all dimensions, pointing to a systemic cause — most likely an ACH rail/processing disruption or calendar effect rather than any segment-specific event.

**Ranked Hypotheses:**

1. **Systemic ACH processing disruption or calendar/banking-window effect** — Confidence: High
   - Evidence: The drop is spread proportionally to baseline share across every dimension. Merchant size: enterprise 43.4%, mid_market 37.4%, smb 19.2% — matches baseline mix. Merchant industry: payroll_staffing 28%, ecommerce 22%, retail 20%, prof_services 15%, healthcare 15% — same shape as baseline. Payer industry, payer size, and tenure all show similarly proportional drops (e.g., tenure: growing 28%, established 25%, loyal 20%, early 19%, new 7%).
   - Mechanism: A platform-wide event (Fed/ACH window holiday or processor incident, partial-day outage, originator file delay) suppresses volume uniformly because every customer routes through the same rail. The 5-day window spanning a weekend (Sept 16–17) plus the typical mid-month payroll lull amplifies the effect.

2. **Mid-month payroll/biller cycle trough (seasonal)** — Confidence: Medium
   - Evidence: payroll_staffing is the largest absolute contributor (-$59K/day, 28% of delta) but only because it is the largest baseline segment (30% of baseline TPV). The drop is *not* over-indexed there. Mid-month (15th–19th) often sits between the 1st/15th and end-of-month payroll surges that dominate ACH.
   - Mechanism: Regular ACH (7-day settle) is heavily used for scheduled payroll and recurring B2B disbursements. A natural lull between cycles would depress volume uniformly across merchants and payers — consistent with the flat decomposition.

3. **Broad macro/customer-spend pullback** — Confidence: Low
   - Evidence: All payer industries (tech, retail_consumer, manufacturing, healthcare, other) dropped 16–28% of delta with no concentration. No tenure cohort over-indexed — even loyal_5plus_yr fell proportionally.
   - Mechanism: A general slowdown would hit everyone, but a 5-day window is too short to attribute to macro conditions; this is more likely a coincident pattern than a real driver.

4. **Targeted churn or fraud event** — Confidence: Low (ruled out)
   - Evidence: No interaction cell exceeds 17% of total delta; top interaction (enterprise merchant × mid_market payer) is just 16.6%. new_0_3mo cohort accounts for only 7% — no acquisition cliff or onboarding fraud signal.
   - Mechanism: Targeted events produce concentrated deltas; that signature is absent here.

**Most Diagnostic Dimension:** `merchant_size` (in combination with the flatness across *all* dimensions)
The decomposition's most informative feature is what it *doesn't* show: no dimension concentrates the delta. Merchant_size comes closest to a signal (enterprise 43%) but its share tracks baseline share, confirming proportionality rather than segment-specific stress.

**Key Segment Findings:**
- `merchant_industry` — **payroll_staffing**: 28.0% of delta (-$59.3K/day) — largest absolute, but proportional to baseline
- `merchant_size` — **enterprise**: 43.4% of delta (-$91.8K/day) — proportional to its 40% baseline share
- `payer_tenure_bucket` — **new_0_3mo**: only 7.3% of delta (-$15.4K/day) — no acquisition or new-payer red flag
- `payer_industry` — **retail_consumer**: 27.8% of delta (-$58.8K/day) — proportional, not concentrated
- Top interaction: `merchant_size × payer_size` — **enterprise × mid_market**: 16.6% of delta — no interaction cell dominates, reinforcing the systemic read

**Recommended Next Steps:**
- [ ] Pull ACH operator logs (NACHA/Fed processing windows) for Sept 15–19; check for file submission delays, return-batch spikes, or originator-side failures
- [ ] Compare same-window daily TPV for `two_day_ach`, `one_day_ach`, and `check` — if other ACH products dropped in parallel, confirms rail-level cause; if only regular_ach, points to product-specific routing
- [ ] Overlay this window against prior Septembers and the broader mid-month vs. end-of-month payroll cycle to quantify the seasonal baseline trough
- [ ] Check transaction counts vs. TPV — is this a count drop (fewer payments) or AOV drop (smaller payments)? Distinguishes cycle effect from customer-behavior shift
- [ ] Notify Payment Ops lead; hold off escalating to VP Finance until rail logs and cross-product comparison confirm or rule out an incident

---

one_day_ach_2024-02-21_2024-02-25.narrative:
---

**TL;DR:** one_day_ach TPV spiked +9.1% (+$98.8K/day) driven almost entirely by enterprise ecommerce merchants, with the ecommerce × enterprise cell alone explaining 158% of the net delta — strongly suggesting a concentrated merchant-level adoption or campaign event rather than a systemic shift.

**Ranked Hypotheses:**

1. **Enterprise ecommerce merchant adoption / campaign surge on one_day_ach** — Confidence: High
   - Evidence: ecommerce contributes +141% of total delta (+$139K/day) while every other merchant industry is *negative*. Enterprise merchants contribute +135% (+$133K/day) while mid_market (-28%) and smb (-7%) declined. The ecommerce × enterprise interaction alone is +$156K/day (158% of delta) — larger than the total anomaly itself.
   - Mechanism: One or a small handful of large ecommerce merchants ramped one_day_ach usage (new merchant going live, promotional volume push, or migration from regular_ach/two_day_ach to expedited rail). The negative contribution from other segments suggests baseline noise/seasonality, not a broad lift.

2. **Payer-side mix shift toward expedited settlement, broad-based** — Confidence: Low-Medium
   - Evidence: All payer industries spiked positively and relatively proportionally (tech +28%, healthcare +26%, retail_consumer +19%, manufacturing +15%, other +12%); all payer sizes spiked; all tenure buckets spiked. This *looks* systemic on the payer side.
   - Mechanism: Could indicate payers electing faster settlement broadly. However, this pattern is more plausibly an artifact of the dominant ecommerce-enterprise merchants serving a diverse payer base — not an independent payer-driven cause.

3. **Cohort onboarding wave in early_4_12mo payers at enterprise merchants** — Confidence: Medium
   - Evidence: early_4_12mo tenure leads payer_tenure deltas (+31%, +$30.9K/day). The enterprise × early_4_12mo interaction is +$40.5K/day (41% of delta), and ecommerce × early_4_12mo is +$40.2K/day (41%).
   - Mechanism: Payers onboarded 4–12 months ago to a large ecommerce enterprise merchant are now hitting steady-state volume on one_day_ach — consistent with a Q3/Q4 2023 onboarding cohort maturing.

4. **Cannibalization from slower ACH rails** — Confidence: Low
   - Evidence: Not directly visible in this report, but the targeted enterprise-ecommerce concentration is consistent with a merchant flipping default rail from regular_ach/two_day_ach to one_day_ach.
   - Mechanism: Need to check whether regular_ach or two_day_ach show a corresponding *drop* in ecommerce × enterprise over the same window.

**Most Diagnostic Dimension:** `merchant_industry × merchant_size`
The interaction cell ecommerce × enterprise accounts for 158% of the total delta — single-handedly larger than the anomaly — while no other merchant segment contributed positively. This pinpoints the anomaly to a narrow merchant cohort, not a platform-wide event.

**Key Segment Findings:**
- `merchant_industry` — **ecommerce**: +141% of total delta (+$139K/day); all other industries negative
- `merchant_size` — **enterprise**: +135% of total delta (+$133K/day); mid_market -28%, smb -7%
- `payer_tenure_bucket` — **early_4_12mo**: +31% of total delta (+$30.9K/day), leading tenure bucket
- Top interaction: `merchant_industry × merchant_size` — **ecommerce × enterprise**: +$156K/day (158% of delta) — the entire anomaly lives here
- Secondary interaction: `merchant_size × payer_tenure_bucket` — **enterprise × early_4_12mo**: +$40.5K/day (41% of delta)

**Recommended Next Steps:**
- [ ] Drill into the merchant_id grain within ecommerce × enterprise for 2024-02-21 to 2024-02-25; identify the top 3–5 merchants by delta and confirm whether one merchant dominates.
- [ ] Pull regular_ach and two_day_ach decompositions for the same window filtered to ecommerce × enterprise to test the rail-migration hypothesis (expect offsetting drops).
- [ ] Check sales/CS notes and merchant configuration change logs for any one_day_ach enablement, pricing change, or go-live event in mid-February for large ecommerce accounts.
- [ ] Validate the early_4_12mo payer cohort: are these payers concentrated under 1–2 ecommerce merchants (consistent with merchant-led onboarding) or distributed?
- [ ] Rule out fraud: confirm transaction counts vs. avg ticket size for the ecommerce × enterprise cell — a large jump in avg ticket with flat count could indicate a few anomalous high-value transfers.
- [ ] Notify Head of Payments and the Enterprise Ecommerce account team; if this is a sustained ramp, update the one_day_ach forecast.

---

one_day_ach_2024-11-25_2024-11-29.narrative:
---

**TL;DR:** one_day_ach TPV spiked +42.1% (+$816K/day) over 2024-11-25 to 11-29, driven overwhelmingly by enterprise ecommerce merchants — consistent with a Black Friday / Cyber Week settlement surge requiring expedited ACH.

**Ranked Hypotheses:**

1. **Black Friday / Cyber Week ecommerce surge on expedited rails** — Confidence: High
   - Evidence: Anomaly window (Nov 25–29) brackets Black Friday (Nov 29). Ecommerce alone drives 71.3% of the delta (+$582K/day); within that, ecommerce × enterprise merchant accounts for 41.0% of total delta (+$335K/day). Enterprise merchants = 56.4% of delta; enterprise payers = 50.9%. Payer industries lean toward tech (28.7%) and retail_consumer (20.6%) — exactly the buyer mix funding ecommerce inventory/marketplace payouts.
   - Mechanism: Large ecommerce platforms shift to one_day_ach during peak holiday week to accelerate merchant/seller payouts and time-sensitive supplier disbursements. Enterprise-on-enterprise flows dominate because large marketplaces process the bulk of holiday GMV.

2. **Product-mix shift: customers upgrading from regular_ach to one_day_ach for peak-week urgency** — Confidence: Medium
   - Evidence: The spike is concentrated in a fast-growing product (+70% YoY) and skews toward established cohorts (growing_1_3yr 27.7%, established_3_5yr 17.5%, loyal_5plus_yr 13.3% — together ~58%), not just new customers. New_0_3mo is only 13.9%, arguing against a pure onboarding wave.
   - Mechanism: Existing customers temporarily route holiday-week payments to the expedited rail; this would show as a corresponding dip or flat trend in regular_ach over the same window.

3. **Concentrated large-merchant behavior (1–2 ecommerce enterprise accounts)** — Confidence: Medium
   - Evidence: The ecommerce × enterprise merchant cell at 41% of total delta is unusually concentrated for an interaction term; ecommerce × tech payer (20.2%) and ecommerce × growing_1_3yr (19.8%) further suggest the surge funnels through a small set of marketplace operators receiving payments from tech-sector buyers.
   - Mechanism: A single large marketplace or platform customer's holiday payout cycle could account for a disproportionate share of the lift. Worth confirming this is broad-based rather than 1–2 accounts.

4. **Fraud or anomalous account activity using expedited rail** — Confidence: Low
   - Evidence: New_0_3mo tenure is the *smallest* contributor (13.9%); the spike is concentrated in established enterprise relationships, not new accounts. Distribution does not match fraud-via-new-accounts pattern.
   - Mechanism: Mostly ruled out, but worth a quick screen given the magnitude on a newer product.

**Most Diagnostic Dimension:** `merchant_industry`
Ecommerce captures 71% of the entire delta — far above its 40% baseline share — and the top five interaction cells all involve ecommerce. This single dimension converts an ambiguous spike into a clearly-categorized holiday/peak-week event.

**Key Segment Findings:**
- `merchant_industry` — **ecommerce**: 71.3% of total delta (+$582K/day)
- `merchant_size` — **enterprise**: 56.4% of total delta (+$460K/day)
- `payer_size` — **enterprise**: 50.9% of total delta (+$416K/day)
- `payer_tenure_bucket` — **early_4_12mo + growing_1_3yr**: 55.3% combined; spike is not new-account driven (new_0_3mo only 13.9%)
- Top interaction: `merchant_industry × merchant_size` — **ecommerce × enterprise**: 41.0% of total delta (+$335K/day) — the single dominant cell

**Recommended Next Steps:**
- [ ] Pull top 10 ecommerce enterprise merchants by one_day_ach volume during Nov 25–29; confirm broad-based vs. concentrated in 1–2 accounts (marketplace IDs).
- [ ] Cross-check regular_ach TPV for the same window — a corresponding flat/declining trend confirms product-mix shift; growth in both confirms net-new holiday volume.
- [ ] Compare against Nov 2023 Black Friday week (if data available) to validate seasonality and quantify YoY one_day_ach holiday lift.
- [ ] Run a fraud/risk screen on any new_0_3mo accounts that contributed disproportionately within ecommerce, even though aggregate signal is low.
- [ ] Notify Head of Payments and Capacity/Ops: confirm rail capacity and funding cover Cyber Monday/Tuesday tail; flag to Finance that this is expected seasonality, not a forecast miss.

---

one_day_ach_2025-04-02_2025-04-03.narrative:
---

**TL;DR:** one_day_ach TPV dropped -50.4% (-$1.16M/day) over a 2-day window with losses spread proportionally across every dimension, strongly indicating a systemic rail or platform incident rather than a segment-specific cause.

**Ranked Hypotheses:**

1. **Payment rail / platform incident affecting one_day_ach** — Confidence: High
   - Evidence: Every segment dropped by ~50% in near-perfect proportion to its baseline share. Merchant industries: ecommerce 39.9%, prof_services 25.1%, healthcare 20.1%, retail 9.9%, payroll 5.0% — these mirror baseline shares (40%, 25%, 20%, 10%, 5%). Same uniformity in payer_industry, payer_size, and tenure. No single segment exceeds 40% of delta on any dimension except enterprise merchant_size (55%, which still tracks its 55% baseline share).
   - Mechanism: A processing outage, rail-level rejection, or feature flag affecting one_day_ach submissions would proportionally suppress volume across all customer cohorts. The 2-day window also fits a discrete incident envelope.

2. **Bank/ODFI partner outage specific to next-day ACH window** — Confidence: Medium
   - Evidence: Drop is isolated to one_day_ach (per this report) and uniform across payers/merchants. Tenure distribution is flat (new through loyal all down ~50%), ruling out cohort behavior.
   - Mechanism: If the originating bank or a specific ACH cutoff/window failed on Apr 2–3, all one_day_ach attempts would either fall back to slower rails or fail outright, hitting all customers equally.

3. **Calendar / holiday or Fed processing day effect** — Confidence: Low-Medium
   - Evidence: April 2–3, 2025 are a Wed/Thu — not a federal holiday, but could coincide with a Fed maintenance window or NACHA processing anomaly. Uniform distribution is consistent.
   - Mechanism: If next-day settlement was unavailable, customers may have shifted to two_day_ach or regular_ach (worth verifying in sibling product reports).

4. **Top-of-funnel pricing/config change on one_day_ach** — Confidence: Low
   - Evidence: Uniformity fits, but a 50% drop in 2 days from a pricing change is unusually sharp and would normally show cohort skew (new payers most price-sensitive). New_0_3mo dropped 14.5%, in line with its 14.5% baseline share — no skew.
   - Mechanism: A fee hike or eligibility tightening would dampen volume but rarely halve it within 48 hours uniformly.

**Most Diagnostic Dimension:** `payer_tenure_bucket`
The tenure distribution is the strongest diagnostic here: every tenure bucket (new through loyal_5plus_yr) dropped by ~50%, with shares of delta within 1–2 points of baseline shares. This rules out churn, acquisition slowdown, and cohort-specific behavior — leaving a platform/rail-level cause.

**Key Segment Findings:**
- `merchant_size` — **enterprise**: 55.4% of total delta (-$642K/day) — tracks 55% baseline share, not over-indexed
- `payer_size` — **enterprise**: 50.3% of total delta (-$583K/day) — proportional to baseline
- `payer_tenure` — **all 5 buckets**: each within 1–2 pts of baseline share; no cohort skew
- `merchant_industry` — **ecommerce**: 39.9% of delta (-$462K/day), matching its 40% baseline share
- Top interaction: `merchant_size × payer_size` — **enterprise × enterprise**: -$324K/day (28% of delta), but this is exactly what a proportional drop predicts given its baseline weight — no intersection effect

**Recommended Next Steps:**
- [ ] Check one_day_ach submission/rejection logs and ODFI partner status for 2025-04-02 to 2025-04-03 (look for elevated reject codes, timeout rates, or auth failures)
- [ ] Pull product engineering change log and feature flag history for one_day_ach in the 48 hours preceding Apr 2
- [ ] Cross-check two_day_ach and regular_ach TPV on the same dates — a compensating spike would confirm rail-fallback behavior; a parallel drop would point to a broader platform issue
- [ ] Verify NACHA / Fed processing calendar and any bank partner maintenance notifications for Apr 2–3
- [ ] Rule out reporting/ETL gap: confirm payment records for Apr 2–3 are fully ingested and not under-counted
- [ ] Notify Head of Payments and on-call Payments Eng; if confirmed as an incident, draft customer comms for affected enterprise merchants

---

check_2025-12-22_2025-12-31.narrative:
---

**TL;DR:** Check TPV spiked +19.1% (+$156K/day) over the Dec 22–31 window, driven overwhelmingly by enterprise merchants receiving enterprise payer checks — consistent with a year-end B2B payment / fiscal-close push rather than a systemic anomaly.

**Ranked Hypotheses:**

1. **Year-end enterprise B2B payment surge (fiscal close / tax-year cutoff)** — Confidence: High
   - Evidence: Enterprise merchants account for 90.7% of the delta (+$141.6K/day); enterprise×enterprise interaction alone is 44.9% of total delta (+$70.0K/day). Spike spans the last 10 days of December. Established (29.3%) and loyal (28.9%) tenure buckets dominate — these are the cohorts that transact via check at year-end. Industry distribution is broad (prof services 32.6%, healthcare 32.0%, retail 16.2%) — not a single-vertical event.
   - Mechanism: Large enterprises rush to clear AP/AR before fiscal/tax year-end, and check remains the preferred instrument for high-value, contract-driven enterprise B2B settlements (vendor true-ups, bonuses, year-end invoices). The concentration in enterprise×enterprise with mature tenure is the classic signature.

2. **Seasonal payroll/bonus and professional-services billing cycle** — Confidence: Medium
   - Evidence: professional_services × enterprise is the #2 interaction at 30.9% (+$48.2K/day); payroll_staffing also spiked (+19% within its segment, +$15.7K/day). Healthcare merchant (+$49.9K/day) aligns with year-end provider settlements.
   - Mechanism: Annual bonus disbursements, retainer true-ups, and year-end professional-services invoicing typically settle by paper check for enterprise clients with established check workflows.

3. **Pull-forward from check decline reversing temporarily** — Confidence: Low-Medium
   - Evidence: Check is a -8% YoY declining product, yet jumped +19% in 10 days. The spike skews to mature tenure (loyal_5plus_yr +28.9%), the cohort least likely to have migrated to ACH.
   - Mechanism: Legacy enterprise payers who still use check for year-end controls produce a seasonal bump that masks the underlying secular decline. Expect reversion in January.

4. **Targeted onboarding or fraud via new accounts** — Confidence: Low (effectively ruled out)
   - Evidence: new_0_3mo contributes only 4.6% of delta; spike is concentrated in established/loyal tenure. No fraud signature.
   - Mechanism: N/A — distribution is inconsistent with fraud.

**Most Diagnostic Dimension:** `merchant_size`
Enterprise merchants explain 90.7% of the total delta while mid-market (6.3%) and SMB (3.0%) are essentially flat. This near-monopoly on the delta — combined with the enterprise×enterprise interaction at 44.9% — pinpoints the spike to large-corporate B2B check flows.

**Key Segment Findings:**
- `merchant_size` — **enterprise**: 90.7% of total delta (+$141.6K/day)
- `payer_size` — **enterprise**: 49.8% of total delta (+$77.7K/day); mid_market 34.6%
- `payer_tenure_bucket` — **established_3_5yr + loyal_5plus_yr**: combined 58.2% (+$90.7K/day); new/early cohorts only 17.4%
- `merchant_industry` — **professional_services (32.6%) + healthcare (32.0%)**: jointly 64.6% of delta, but spike is broad across all 5 industries
- Top interaction: `merchant_size × payer_size` — **enterprise × enterprise**: 44.9% of delta (+$70.0K/day) — the anomaly is concentrated at the large-corporate intersection.

**Recommended Next Steps:**
- [ ] Pull top-20 enterprise merchant check receipts for Dec 22–31 vs. same window 2024 to confirm year-over-year seasonality (rule out a one-off anomaly).
- [ ] Compare against Dec 2024 and Dec 2023 check TPV — if a similar Dec 22–31 spike exists historically, classify as seasonal and update baseline detection to exclude fiscal year-end.
- [ ] Query AP system / check issuance logs for enterprise×enterprise payers to identify the top contributing payer-merchant pairs and confirm payment memos (bonus, year-end invoice, true-up).
- [ ] Verify ACH products (regular/two_day/one_day) did NOT show a corresponding drop — would rule out a channel-shift hypothesis.
- [ ] Notify Head of Payments and Finance that January TPV is expected to revert; do not extrapolate this run-rate into Q1 forecasts.

---
